_meta.purpose: 2026 income tax brackets for the four states whose legacy entries carry a `type` and `topRate` but no bracket array. Without these the engine loops over an empty bracket list and returns $0, so every page told residents of Maryland, Nebraska, South Carolina and West Virginia that they owed no state income tax.
_meta.researchedBy: web research against state revenue departments and cross-checked against independent tax references, July 2026
_meta.appliedBy: scripts/gen-data.mjs merges this into src/data/states.ts at generation time. legacy/ is read-only and is never edited; states.ts is generated and is never hand-edited.
rules: Every bracket below is transcribed from a cited source. Nothing is interpolated, rounded or inferred.
Where sources disagreed, the state's own revenue department wins. Two such conflicts were found and are recorded per state below.
Fields that could not be independently confirmed are omitted rather than guessed — see Maryland's standard deduction.
sources: West Virginia Tax Division — 2026 Income Tax Rate Cut (tax.wv.gov/Individuals/Pages/PersonalIncomeTaxReductionBill.aspx)
South Carolina Department of Revenue — Information about H. 4216 (dor.sc.gov/news/information-about-h-4216)
Nebraska Legislature — Neb. Rev. Stat. 77-2715.03 (statutory rates and indexing rule)
Tax Foundation — State Individual Income Tax Rates and Brackets, 2026
ustax.tools — Nebraska / Maryland 2026 bracket tables (independent cross-check)

MD._confidence: high on brackets, standard deduction deliberately omitted
MD._note: Bracket structure agreed across Tax Foundation 2026 and multiple independent references, and matches the legacy dataset's own note that the 2025 BRF Act added the 6.25% and 6.5% brackets. topRate 6.5% was already correct.
MD._omitted: No standardDeduction is set. Sources disagree ($2,550 vs $3,350) because Maryland's standard deduction is a percentage of AGI with a floor and a cap, which this engine cannot express as a single figure. Omitting it means Maryland tax is computed on full AGI and is therefore slightly OVERSTATED — the page says so. Do not paper over this with a guessed number.
MD._localTax: Maryland county tax (2.25%-3.20%) is separate and is not part of these brackets; it is surfaced from stateMetadata.statesWithLocalIncomeTax.
MD.dataCaveat: Maryland's standard deduction is a percentage of adjusted gross income with a floor and a cap, which this calculator cannot express, so it is not applied. Maryland tax shown here is computed on full income and is therefore slightly overstated.

NE._confidence: high on rates (statute), medium-high on thresholds (two independent references agreeing, not the DOR directly)
NE._note: Neb. Rev. Stat. 77-2715.03 sets four statutory brackets but rate three and rate four are BOTH 4.55% for tax years beginning on or after 2026-01-01, so the top two collapse into one. That is why some references say three brackets and the statute says four — they agree. One reference quoted a 4.40% middle bracket and thresholds of $3,990/$23,930; those are the pre-indexation figures and are not used.
NE._thresholds: Bracket dollar amounts are inflation-indexed annually by the Tax Commissioner under 77-2715.03. The 2026 values below come from Tax Foundation 2026 and are corroborated by an independent bracket table.

SC._confidence: high — primary source is the South Carolina Department of Revenue
SC._note: H. 4216, signed 2026-03-30, replaced the three-bracket schedule with two brackets effective for tax year 2026. SC DOR states it as '1.99% under $30,000' and '5.21% from $30,000 and above, minus $966'. The $966 offset is an equivalent formulation of a marginal bracket, not an extra deduction: at $30,000 both forms give $597 (30,000 x 1.99% = 597; 30,000 x 5.21% - 966 = 597), and at $50,000 both give $1,639. Transcribed below as the equivalent marginal brackets so the engine needs no special case.
SC._supersedes: Tax Foundation's 2026 table still shows the OLD 0%/3%/6% schedule for South Carolina. It is out of date; the DOR is authoritative.

WV._confidence: high — primary source is the West Virginia Tax Division
WV._note: A 5% across-the-board cut took effect 2026-06-12, retroactive to 2026-01-01, so these are the correct figures for the whole 2026 tax year. Internally consistent with the Tax Division's own cumulative amounts: $211 at $10,000; $632.50 at $25,000; $1,106.50 at $40,000; $1,950.50 at $60,000.
WV._supersedes: Tax Foundation's 2026 table shows the pre-cut schedule (2.22/2.96/3.33/4.44/4.82). Those rates were correct until 12 June 2026 and are now superseded. The legacy dataset's topRate of 4.82% is from that same pre-cut schedule and is corrected to 4.58% below.

IN._confidence: high — Indiana DOR is the primary source and a second search confirmed it
IN._note: The legacy dataset carries 3.00%, which was the 2025 rate. Indiana's 2026 rate is 2.95%, dropping again to 2.90% in 2027. The legacy note ('Rate has been decreasing') was right about the direction and stale on the figure.
sources: in.gov/dor Rates, Fees and Penalties — 2026 individual AGI rate 2.95%, 2027 2.90%
